# Next generation lineage tracing and its applications to unravel development

[*npj Systems Biology and Applications*](/npjsba)
**volume 11**, Article number: 60 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

2771 Accesses

[Metrics details](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Lineage tracing remains an essential approach towards understanding cell fate, tissue formation, and human development. Herein, we review advancements in lineage tracing techniques, the integration of sequencing and imaging technologies, and computational tools for analysis. We report on recent lineage tracing applications, including integrative techniques (MADM-CloneSeq), in situ hybridization (DART-FISH), and modern workflows (BaSISS), which hold an essential role in our aim to unravel lineage hierarchies and, ultimately, human development.

## Introduction

Lineage tracing cannot be defined in terms of a single technique or approach, but as any experimental design aimed at establishing hierarchical relationships between cells. Modern flagship studies are rigorous and multimodal, validating hypotheses by a multitude of distinct methods. It is not unusual for such studies to incorporate advanced microscopy, state-of-the-art sequencing technology, and multiple biological models. The resulting datasets continue to increase in size and complexity, necessitating sophisticated and integrative approaches to experimental design and analysis. However, when broken into constituent parts, these studies are rooted in the same fundamental concepts that define all lineage-tracing studies. In brief, this review introduces lineage tracing, examines key historical developments, highlights recent advancements in imaging-based lineage-tracing techniques (new and old), examines up-to-date analytical and modelling-based tools, and provides an outlook on an integrative future of lineage tracing.

Studies applying lineage tracing may investigate cell/tissue morphology, differentiation, clonal expansion, and gene inheritance/expression. The resolution and methodological approach will define the limits of an analysis, in either precision or generalisability. For example, the low specificity of a particular label may prevent discrimination between particular cell types. Alternatively, excessive labelling can lead to close proximity of clonal populations in situ, limiting a study’s ability to perform clonal analysis. Experimental constraints such as these often restrict a study to making inferences about a cell ‘population’, which may not represent a uniform population of cells, but a group of cells sharing a particular trait (a shared phenotype, gene-of-interest, etc.). For example, the transcription factor Sox9 identifies osteochondral stem and progenitor cells, remaining present until after commitment to the chondrocyte lineage[1](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR1 "Lefebvre, V., Angelozzi, M. & Haseeb, A. SOX9 in cartilage development and disease. Curr. Opin. Cell Biol. 61, 39–47 (2019).")—as such, to discuss ‘Sox9+ cells’ would not refer to a single-cell type, but a spectrum of cell types with a shared marker. This is not to imply that higher specificity is unilaterally superior; population-level analyses assist in generalising observations made in single cells (or cell types) or forming associations between discriminant cell types.

Although rooted in developmental biology, lineage tracing has been implemented to answer biological questions for a wide range of topics. Lineage-tracing data can provide insight into cellular origins, proliferation, and differentiation. Depending on experimental design, lineage tracing can answer questions about regenerative models, cancer development, and disease progression. In regeneration, lineage tracing has established cell origin and fate in models spanning embryonic development to adult tissues. Tracing cancer cell lineages has determined mutations critical to cancer progression and lineage-specificity for therapeutics. In disease models, these same techniques are used to measure changes in cell potential and stem cell exhaustion.

Over the past twenty years, non-imaging tools (e.g., scRNAseq) have become a vital tool in formulating and validating lineage-tracing hypotheses[2](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR2 "Chen, C., Liao, Y. & Peng, G. Connecting past and present: single-cell lineage tracing. Protein Cell 13, 790–807 (2022)."),[3](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR3 "Wagner, D. E. & Klein, A. M. Lineage tracing meets single-cell omics: opportunities and challenges. Nat. Rev. Genet. 21, 410–427 (2020)."). As sequencing technologies improve in their power, accessibility, and cost, what were once expensive and exceptional approaches are increasingly becoming a requisite tool in modern lineage tracing work. Despite this, traditional imaging-based approaches remain central to lineage-tracing studies. When investigating less-studied cell lineages (e.g., skeletal or epithelial stem cells, cutting-edge technologies are frequently not applicable, either due to unavailability or an insufficient understanding of the underlying lineage hierarchy. Therefore, it is important as researchers to have a comprehensive understanding of not only the technological cutting-edge, but the foundational techniques on which our field is built and are largely still applicable and often necessary. Since its conception, lineage tracing has remained of central importance in biology, and imaging has persisted as the fundamental tool in performing lineage tracing both qualitatively and quantitatively. Herein, we highlight recent advancements and implementations of imaging-based lineage-tracing techniques (new and old), provide context in contrast to previous iterations, and offer readers a concise summary of how these techniques have and/or may be applied.

### A brief historical perspective

Lineage tracing has remained of central importance in biology since the late 1800s, when Charles Whitman reported the direct observation of germ layer differentiation in leeches[4](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR4 "Whitman, C. O. A contribution to the history of the germ-layers in Clepsine. J. Morphol. 1, 105–182 (1887)."). In 1887, direct observation was the sole method for interpreting cell lineage, and data collection was ultimately dependent on the visual observations of an experimenter in real time. As a result, experimental models would have been limited to those that afforded observable changes via light microscopy (Box [1](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#Sec3))[4](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR4 "Whitman, C. O. A contribution to the history of the germ-layers in Clepsine. J. Morphol. 1, 105–182 (1887)."),[5](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR5 "Conklin, E. G. The embryology of crepidula, a contribution to the cell lineage and early development of some marine gasteropods. J. Morphol. 13, 1–226 (1897)."). Cell arrangement, differentiation events, and counts would have been visually monitored and recorded manually.

Labelling allows experimenters to distinguish and monitor cells by means other than continuous observation, and has greatly increased in sophistication since its conception. Non-specific labels were originally applied to lineage tracing by Eric Vogt, who fate mapped an amphibian blastula in 1929 using Nile Blue[6](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR6 "Vogt, W. Gestaltungsanalyse am Amphibienkeim mit Örtlicher Vitalfärbung. Wilhelm. Roux Arch. Entwickl. Mech. Org. 120, 384–706 (1929)."). A more modern implementation of non-specific labelling is nucleoside analogues (BrdU, EdU, etc.), in which a modified nucleoside is incorporated into cellular DNA and is subsequently labelled with fluorescent dye[7](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR7 "Miller, M. W. & Nowakowski, R. S. Use of bromodeoxyuridine-immunohistochemistry to examine the proliferation, migration and time of origin of cells in the central nervous system. Brain Res. 457, 44–52 (1988)."),[8](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR8 "Nowakowski, R. S., Lewin, S. B. & Miller, M. W. Bromodeoxyuridine immunohistochemical determination of the lengths of the cell cycle and the DNA-synthetic phase for an anatomically defined population. J. Neurocytol. 18, 311–318 (1989)."). Nucleoside analogues are applied to identify proliferating cell populations, which will incorporate the dye more quickly than slow-cycling populations and are still frequently used today, with the natural disadvantage of label dilution proportional to cell proliferation.

The late 20th century was marked by the exponential development of gene editing technologies, which were particularly useful in refining imaging methodologies for lineage analysis. By manipulating the expression of reporter genes, labelling technologies circumvented many of the limitations posed by earlier approaches. Starting in the 1980s, the first transgenic approaches involving enzymatic reporters were produced[9](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR9 "Shuman, H. A., Silhavy, T. J. & Beckwith, J. R. Labeling of proteins with beta-galactosidase by gene fusion. Identification of a cytoplasmic membrane component of the Escherichia coli maltose transport system. J. Biol. Chem. 255, 168–174 (1980)."), a key example of which is the *E. coli*-derived β-galactosidase (which can convert substrate X-gal into a dark blue precipitate)[10](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR10 "Pardy, K. in Methods in Molecular Biology 18 (eds. Murphy, D. & Carter, D. A.) 419–424 (Humana Press, Totowa, NJ, 1993)."). At this time, the expanding literature on transgenic modalities allowed for gene incorporation either by direct injection, viral transduction, or plasmid transfection by 1990[11](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR11 "Stuhlmann, H., Cone, R., Mulligan, R. C. & Jaenisch, R. Introduction of a selectable gene into different animal tissue by a retrovirus recombinant vector. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 81, 7151–7155 (1984)."),[12](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR12 "Holt, C. E., Garlick, N. & Cornel, E. Lipofection of cDNAs in the embryonic vertebrate central nervous system. Neuron 4, 203–214 (1990)."). The Cre-loxP recombinase system was also introduced during this period, being implemented in mammalian cells in 1988[13](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR13 "Sauer, B. & Henderson, N. Site-specific DNA recombination in mammalian cells by the Cre recombinase of bacteriophage P1. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 85, 5166–5170 (1988)."), and in mice in vivo in 1994[14](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR14 "Gu, H., Marth, J. D., Orban, P. C., Mossmann, H. & Rajewsky, K. Deletion of a DNA polymerase β gene segment in T cells using cell type-specific gene targeting. Science 265, 103–106 (1994)."). The year 1994 also saw the introduction of green fluorescent protein (GFP) as an endogenous reporter, which marked a massive shift for lineage tracing, giving cells the potential to express reporters without the need for an external stimulus[15](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR15 "Chalfie, M., Tu, Y., Euskirchen, G., Ward, W. W. & Prasher, D. C. Green fluorescent protein as a marker for gene expression. Science 263, 802–805 (1994)."),[16](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR16 "Rodriguez, E. A. et al. The growing and glowing toolbox of fluorescent and photoactive proteins. Trends Biochem Sci. 42, 111–129 (2017)."). Taken together, these tools laid the technological foundation for lineage tracing in the modern era (Fig. [1](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#Fig1)).

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41540-025-00542-w/MediaObjects/41540_2025_542_Fig1_HTML.png)

Key technological and methodological advances have driven large shifts in progress since the 1800s (**a**). Results returned from a PubMed search of the term “lineage tracing” (**b**). Timeline highlighting key discoveries that have laid the foundation for modern lineage tracing (**c**). Created using BioRender.

### Box 1 Landmark microscope technology contextualises the genesis of fate mapping

Early studies into cell lineage were predated by several key advancements in light microscopy. In the mid to late 1800s, efforts from Carl Zeiss, Ernst Abbe, and Otto Schott led to the development of high-performance light microscopes with optimised resolutions nearing the diffraction limit of light (~ 200 nm)—a limit which would not be broken until 1994[80](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR80 "ARAKI, T. The history of optical microscope. Mech. Eng. Rev. 4, 16-00242–16-00242 (2017)."). Of note, stand-based microscopes were developed for the first time (1857), Abbe’s theory led to the development of the apochromat lens, which corrected for chromatic aberration (1873), and oil immersion lenses were developed to offer greater magnification (1877)[80](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR80 "ARAKI, T. The history of optical microscope. Mech. Eng. Rev. 4, 16-00242–16-00242 (2017)."). These advances would serve to push the technological limit of microscopy until the development of the confocal microscope in 1957[81](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR81 "Minsky, M. Memoir on inventing the confocal scanning microscope. Scanning 10, 128–138 (1988)."), and provide the means for the burst of imaging-based technologies to come.

## Image-based lineage-tracing techniques

### Site-specific recombinases

Central to imaging-based lineage-tracing research are site-specific recombinase (SSR) systems, of which Cre-loxP remains one of the most fundamental and commonly used. The widespread availability, versatility, and ease of use all uphold the Cre-loxP system as the gold standard in lineage-tracing studies. These systems can be used to knock-in/knock-out alleles and influence gene expression with a great degree of cell and temporal specificity. In the context of lineage tracing, Cre-loxP systems are commonly applied in clonal analysis studies, during which Cre recombinase often excises a STOP codon between two adjacent loxP binding sites, activating a fluorescent reporter gene. The specificity of this activation is dependent on Cre, whose expression can be driven by cell-type-specific promoters or ubiquitously expressed. These approaches traditionally consist of a single fluorescent reporter tied to a single gene of interest, the concepts of which have been reviewed at length in the literature[17](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR17 "Kim, H., Kim, M., Im, S.-K. & Fang, S. Mouse Cre-LoxP system: general principles to determine tissue-specific roles of target genes. Lab. Anim. Res. 34, 147 (2018)."). However, the Cre-loxP system continues to be applied to develop advanced lineage-tracing techniques, including dual SSR systems, multicolour models, and more advanced methods.

Analyses using single fluorescent reporters can provide population data but are largely limited in their ability to resolve cell populations at the single-cell level. This largely stems from the difficulty (and often impossibility) of distinguishing clonal groups within a homogenously labelled population. Despite this, sparse labelling approaches can be used to circumvent this. For example, in sparse labelling, the activating agent in inducible models (e.g., Tamoxifen in CreERT2 models) can be titrated such that recombination is limited to a limited number of cells within the population (thus allowing for spatial separation)[18](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR18 "Tan, S. H. & Nusse, R. In vivo lineage tracing reveals Axin2-expressing, long-lived cortical thymic epithelial progenitors in the postnatal thymus. PLoS ONE 12, e0184582 (2017)."). This has the added effect of increased specificity, as cells with greater promoter expression will be preferentially labelled[19](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR19 "Hsu, Y.-C., Pasolli, H. A. & Fuchs, E. Dynamics between Stem Cells, Niche, and Progeny in the Hair Follicle. Cell 144, 92–105 (2011)."). Unfortunately, the natural limitation to sparse labelling approaches is the natural increase in sampling required (meaning increased biological replicates, analyses, and lower reproducibility).

### Dual recombinase systems

The Cre-loxP system can also be implemented in combination with analogous technologies to create dual recombinase systems. Dre-rox is a common heterospecific and efficient alternative to Cre-loxP in which Dre recombinase is specific for rox sites[20](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR20 "Sauer, B. DNA recombination with a heterospecific Cre homolog identified from comparison of the pac-c1 regions of P1-related phages. Nucleic Acids Res. 32, 6086–6095 (2004)."),[21](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR21 "Anastassiadis, K. et al. Dre recombinase, like Cre, is a highly efficient site-specific recombinase in E. coli, mammalian cells and mice. Dis. Model Mech. 2, 508–515 (2009)."). Dual recombinase systems take advantage of the site specificity of recombinases and offer multiple experimental design strategies beneficial to lineage tracing (i.e., expression occurs following recombination of (i) either Cre or Dre, (ii) both Cre and Dre, (iii) Cre in the absence of Dre)[22](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR22 "Li, H., Weng, W. & Zhou, B. Perfect duet: dual recombinases improve genetic resolution. Cell Prolif. 56, e13446 (2023)."). Several dual systems have been implemented, Cre-loxP/Dre-rox being one of the most common, and possess numerous applications in lineage tracing. For example, a Cre/Dre dual system was recently used to determine the origin of regenerative cells in remodelled bone, distinguishing otherwise homogenous periosteal tissue into distinct layers and evaluating layer contributions in fracture regeneration[23](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR23 "Liu, Y. L., Tang, X. T., Shu, H. S., Zou, W. & Zhou, B. O. Fibrous periosteum repairs bone fracture and maintains the healed bone throughout mouse adulthood. Dev. Cell 59, 1192–1209.e6 (2024)."). Cre/Dre was also applied to investigate the cellular origins of alveolar epithelial stem cells post-injury and distinguish contributions made by multiple epithelial cell populations simultaneously[24](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR24 "Liu, K. et al. Tracing the origin of alveolar stem cells in lung repair and regeneration. Cell 187, 2428–2445.e20 (2024)."). Dual recombinases were also applied to develop novel genetic techniques for evaluating senescence during liver fibrosis, offering an approach to discriminating between senescent cell populations expressing analogous senescent markers[25](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR25 "Zhao, H. et al. Identifying specific functional roles for senescence across cell types. Cell 187, 7314–7334.e21 (2024).").

### Multicolour lineage-tracing approaches

A major advance in imaging-based lineage tracing was the introduction of multicolour reporter cassettes. The first of these was ‘Brainbow’, capable of expressing up to four different fluorescent proteins and driven by stochastic Cre-loxP-mediated excision and/or inversion[26](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR26 "Livet, J. et al. Transgenic strategies for combinatorial expression of fluorescent proteins in the nervous system. Nature 450, 56–62 (2007)."). In this design, multiple pairs of loxP sites are arranged within the cassette, facilitating mutually exclusive recombination events. Recombination will ultimately result in a reordering/removal of fluorescent protein sequences and—given only the first fluorophore in sequence will be transcribed—expression of a different fluorescent protein. This technology has since been adapted to several experimental models and inspired numerous analogous technologies[27](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR27 "Weissman, T. A. & Pan, Y. A. Brainbow: new resources and emerging biological applications for multicolor genetic labeling and analysis. Genetics 199, 293–306 (2015)."),[28](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR28 "Dumas, L., Clavreul, S., Michon, F. & Loulier, K. Multicolor strategies for investigating clonal expansion and tissue plasticity. Cell. Mol. Life Sci. 79, 141 (2022)."). One of the most popular adaptations is the R26R-Confetti reporter, given its widespread applicability to existing Cre models. Lineage-tracing studies now incorporate confetti reporters as a means to perform clonal analysis at the single-cell level in a broad range of tissues, including hematopoetic[29](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR29 "Nicolai, L. et al. Single platelet and megakaryocyte morpho-dynamics uncovered by multicolor reporter mouse strains in vitro and in vivo. Haematologica 107, 1669–1680 (2021)."), epithelial[30](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR30 "Ohmoto, M. et al. Maintenance and turnover of Sox2+ adult stem cells in the gustatory epithelium. PLoS ONE 17, e0267683 (2022)."), kidney[31](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR31 "Yin, J. et al. Fate mapping reveals compartment-specific clonal expansion of mononuclear phagocytes during kidney disease. Kidney Int. 104, 605–610 (2023)."), and skeletal[32](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR32 "Newton, P. T. et al. A radical switch in clonality reveals a stem cell niche in the epiphyseal growth plate. Nature 567, 234–238 (2019).") cells. Multicolour models are also being applied in live-imaging studies, for example, confetti reporters have recently been used in intravital imaging to trace macrophage origin and proliferation in mammary glands in real time[33](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR33 "Dawson, C. A. et al. Tissue-resident ductal macrophages survey the mammary epithelium and facilitate tissue remodelling. Nat. Cell Biol. 22, 546–558 (2020)."). The protocol used in this study was subsequently published by the authors and carries promising applications in future organogenesis studies[34](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR34 "Dawson, C. A., Mueller, S. N., Lindeman, G. J., Rios, A. C. & Visvader, J. E. Intravital microscopy of dynamic single-cell behavior in mouse mammary tissue. Nat. Protoc. 16, 1907–1935 (2021).").

Mosaic analysis with a repressible cell marker (MARCM) is a tracing technique that identifies lineage tree branches based on mitotic recombination. Originally developed in *Drosophila*, MARCM uses the FLP-FRT system (analogous to Cre-loxP) to induce recombination between homologous chromosomes during mitosis, ultimately resulting in a single daughter cell inheriting a reporter[35](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR35 "Lee, T. & Luo, L. Mosaic analysis with a repressible cell marker for studies of gene function in neuronal morphogenesis. Neuron 22, 451–461 (1999)."). Pre-recombination, a ubiquitously expressed transcriptional repressor prevents reporter expression; following mitotic recombination, the repressor and reporter genes are partitioned into corresponding daughter cells by chromosomal segregation. This technology was improved to incorporate two repressor/reporter pairs (twin-spot MARCM) and analogously developed for the Cre-loxP system as mosaic analysis with double markers (MADM)[36](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR36 "Yu, H.-H., Chen, C.-H., Shi, L., Huang, Y. & Lee, T. Twin-spot MARCM to reveal the developmental origin and identity of neurons. Nat. Neurosci. 12, 947–953 (2009)."),[37](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR37 "Zong, H., Espinosa, J. S., Su, H. H., Muzumdar, M. D. & Luo, L. Mosaic analysis with double markers in mice. Cell 121, 479–492 (2005)."). Both systems offer unparalleled specificity in fate mapping by labelling branches of a lineage tree in a mitosis-dependent manner. Recombination events exclusively occur during mitosis, and daughter cells are asymmetrically labelled, meaning lineage branches are labelled at their origin point and sibling cells can be independently traced. A major limitation of mitotic recombination systems was their availability for each chromosome, but as of 2021, a genome-wide library for MADM is available, with knock-in cassettes for all 19 autosomes[38](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR38 "Contreras, X. et al. A genome-wide library of MADM mice for single-cell genetic mosaic analysis. Cell Rep. 35, 109274 (2021)."). MADM has now facilitated highly specific lineage tracing of neural stem and progenitor cells during cortical development[39](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR39 "Laukoter, S. et al. Imprinted Cdkn1c genomic locus cell-autonomously promotes cell survival in cerebral cortex development. Nat. Commun. 11, 195 (2020)."),[40](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR40 "Amberg, N., Pauler, F. M., Streicher, C. & Hippenmeyer, S. Tissue-wide genetic and cellular landscape shapes the execution of sequential PRC2 functions in neural stem cell lineage progression. Sci. Adv. 8, eabq1263 (2022).") and seed populations responsible for breast and ovarian cancer (these and other applications are summarised in Table [1](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#Tab1))[41](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR41 "Zeng, J. et al. Dichotomous ovarian cancer-initiating potential of Pax8+ cells revealed by a mouse genetic mosaic model. iScience 26, 106742 (2023)."),[42](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR42 "Zeng, J. et al. A genetic mosaic mouse model illuminates the pre-malignant progression of basal-like breast cancer. Dis. Model Mech. 16, dmm05019 (2023)."). MADM-based clonal analysis has recently been paired with single-cell sequencing techniques (MADM-CloneSeq) to perform lineage tracing of the murine midbrain at the single-cell level[43](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR43 "Cheung, G. et al. Multipotent progenitors instruct ontogeny of the superior colliculus. Neuron 112, 230–246.e11 (2024)."). In this multimodal technique, imaging is used to identify clones (via MADM), after which RNA is isolated from individual cells-of-interest for sequencing. Techniques such as MADM-CloneSeq bridge the gap between imaging and sequencing technologies—an approach built on foundational technologies that will likely prove versatile in a broad range of models. In parallel to Cre and other SSR-based approaches, advances in the field of image-based spatial transcriptomics have expanded our ability to spatiotemporally visualise transcriptional targets in situ.

## Spatial transcriptomics and its applications to lineage tracing

### In situ hybridisation

In situ hybridisation (ISH) encompasses a broad range of techniques based on detecting nucleic acid (NA) sequences (DNA or RNA) of interest through complementary binding of labelled single-stranded sequences. Once bound, probes may then be detected in situ during imaging. Originally developed in the late 1960s, early approaches to ISH involved autoradiography of tritium (3H)-incorporated RNA sequences[44](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR44 "Gall, J. G. & Pardue, M. L. Formation and detection of RNA-DNA hybrid molecules in cytological preparations. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 63, 378–383 (1969)."). The advent of fluorescent probes together with technological advancements in microscopy led to the development of fluorescent in situ hybridisation (FISH). The early 1980s saw two major advancements to ISH, in which (1) detection of biotin-labelled probes by immunofluorescence[45](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR45 "Langer-Safer, P. R., Levine, M. & Ward, D. C. Immunological method for mapping genes on Drosophila polytene chromosomes. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 79, 4381–4385 (1982).") and (2) direct fluorescent labelling of RNA probes[46](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR46 "Bauman, J. G. J., Wiegant, J., Borst, P. & van Duijn, P. A new method for fluorescence microscopical localization of specific DNA sequences by in situ hybridization of fluorochrome-labelled RNA. Exp. Cell Res. 128, 485–490 (1980).") were used to detect NA sequences. The decades that followed saw the development of single-molecule FISH (smFISH), enabling the detection of individual copies of mRNA[47](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR47 "Femino, A. M., Fay, F. S., Fogarty, K. & Singer, R. H. Visualization of single RNA transcripts in situ. Science (1979) 280, 585–590 (1998)."),[48](/articles/s41540-025-00542-w#ref-CR48 "Raj, A., van den Bogaard, P., Rifkin, S. A., van Oudenaarden, A. & Tyagi, S. Imaging individual mRNA molecules using multiple singly labeled probes.

Recent advancements in single-cell RNA sequencing, spatial transcriptomics, and multi-omics integration have enabled researchers to dissect complex tissues and identify distinct cell populations with unique functional states. This review discusses the application of single-cell omics in diverse fields, including cancer research and developmental biology, showcasing how they reveal insights into cellular interactions, disease mechanisms, and therapeutic responses. Notable studies illustrate the potential of single-cell approaches to uncover novel biomarkers and therapeutic targets, particularly in heterogeneous diseases such as cancer and neurodevelopmental disorders. Furthermore, the review emphasizes the importance of integrating single-cell data with computational models to enhance our understanding of cellular dynamics and microenvironmental influences. Overall, this review underscores the critical role of single-cell omics in advancing our knowledge of biology and its applications in clinical settings, paving the way for personalized medicine.

Introduction
------------

Understanding cellular heterogeneity, the diversity in cell types, states, and functions within a biological system is fundamental to advancing biology and medicine. Single-cell omics emerged as a cutting-edge technique in molecular biology that combines multiple types of omics data types, such as genomics, transcriptomics, epigenomics, and proteomics from individual cells. Unlike traditional bulk methods, which average measurements across large cell populations, single-cell omics capture the heterogeneity within individual cells, providing a detailed view of cellular states, functions, and interactions ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig1)a) [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib1)]. For example, in cancer research, single-cell omics has revolutionized cancer research by providing unprecedented insights into tumor biology, heterogeneity, microenvironment interactions, and the mechanisms driving cancer progression, metastasis, and therapy resistance [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib2)]. Developmental biology also benefits, with trajectory tools tracing lineage progression and regulatory shifts during differentiation [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib3)].

Beyond basic research, single-cell approaches are transforming clinical science [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib4)]. For instance, single-cell omics has been used to study breast cancer [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib5)], autoimmune diseases [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib6)], neurological disorders [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib7)], developmental disorders [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib8)], cardiovascular diseases [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib9)] and infectious diseases [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib10)], revealing insights into cell subpopulations, immune cell interactions, and disease-specific gene expression changes. These advancements provide a comprehensive view of cellular processes at unprecedented resolution, paving the way for personalized medicine and targeted therapies.

This review explores single-cell omics techniques ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig1)a), and their utility in basic and translational research ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig1)b), such as _decoding cellular heterogeneity, reconstructing gene regulatory networks, single-cell functional analysis through perturbation, cell lineage trajectory reconstruction, cell–cell communication, drug repurposing_, and success stories in clinical trials ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig1)c). These advancements are driving discoveries and inspiring new therapies, bridging basic research and clinical innovation.

Combining single-cell omics techniques for basic and translational research
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

The combination of scRNA-seq with other techniques is revolutionizing basic and translational research. For example, the single-cell multiome technique from 10X Genomics, can jointly profile gene expression (scRNA-seq) as well as chromatin accessibility (scATAC-seq) from the same single-cell ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#tbl1)). Similarly, single-cell SNMT-seq is a multi-omics sequencing technique that allows simultaneous profiling of three critical layers of gene regulation i.e. chromatin accessibility, DNA methylation, and gene expression at the single-cell level. Similarly, several other techniques have been developed combining multiomics profiles to provide much deeper insights into the molecular changes at single-cell resolution ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#tbl1)). Furthermore, recent advancements in spatial technologies enable the integration of single-cell resolution with spatial context, allowing researchers to analyze multiple molecular layers—including the genome, transcriptome, proteome, and epigenome within their native tissue environment. For instance, NanoString's GeoMx DSP has been instrumental in uncovering inter- and intra-tumoral heterogeneity, mapping the spatial organization of tumors into distinct cellular communities, and identifying treatment-induced remodelling. In pancreatic cancer, this technology has provided clinically relevant prognostic insights, paving the way for more precise therapeutic strategies [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib11)]. Similarly, a recent study utilized the Xenium platform [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib12)]∗∗, coupled with single-cell analyses, providing a deeper understanding of tumor heterogeneity and treatment response in brain cancer [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib13)]. Though these methods offer advanced spatial transcriptomics capabilities, it comes with several limitations such as limited resolution, low transcript coverage, tissue preparation challenges, limited multi-Omics integration, higher cost, and high computational requirements [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib14)].

Over the past decade, the routine application of scRNA-seq on large-scale cellular populations has profoundly advanced our understanding of the inherent heterogeneity within complex biological systems. Meanwhile, independent breakthroughs in mass spectrometry (MS) technologies have brought us closer to achieving high-resolution single-cell proteome characterization, further bridging the gap between transcriptomics and proteomics at the single-cell level. Several MS techniques such as SCoPE-MS (Single-Cell Proteomics by Mass Spectrometry) [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib15)], SCoPE2 [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib16)], and nanoPOTS (nanodroplet processing in one-pot) [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib17)], have been developed for scientific research. Similarly, antibody-based approaches such as CyTOF (Mass Cytometry) and imaging-based proteomics techniques like Imaging Mass Cytometry (IMC) have enabled the high-dimensional visualization of normal and diseased tissues in situ.

The integration of these cutting-edge technologies hold immense potential in exploring immune cell characterization, developmental biology, and disease pathology, offering unprecedented insights into cellular mechanisms. By capturing intricate molecular and spatial interactions, these approaches are reshaping our understanding of complex biological systems and paving the way for innovative therapeutic strategies [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib18)].∗.

Unraveling cellular heterogeneity with single-cell omics
--------------------------------------------------------

Cellular heterogeneity refers to the diversity of cell types, states, and functions within a tissue, organ, or biological system—an essential characteristic of multicellular organisms ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig2)a) [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib19)]. Understanding this diversity is crucial for deciphering complex biological processes, including development, immune responses, and disease progression. Single-cell omics technologies enable the identification of previously unknown cell types, developmental trajectories, and cell–state transitions, offering transformative insights into cellular biology. For instance, a study employing single-cell multiomics (CITE-seq) profiled leukemic stem cells (LSCs) and progenitors, revealing their dynamic molecular heterogeneity and its role in variable therapeutic responses [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib20)]∗∗. Similarly, spatial and single-cell transcriptomic profiling of the lymph node microenvironment in intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma uncovered key mechanisms driving metastasis and tissue remodeling [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib21)].

Recent innovations, such as Dyna-vivo-seq, introduced by Yin et al. (2024), utilize in vivo metabolic RNA labeling to track cellular RNA dynamics during acute kidney injury (AKI). This scRNA-seq approach provides a powerful tool for studying temporal transcription dynamics at a single-cell resolution, offering key insights into cellular responses and gene expression changes over time [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib22)].

Furthermore, integrative analysis of spatial and single-cell transcriptomics has provided a high-resolution view of papillary thyroid cancer (PTC) evolution, shedding light on tumor heterogeneity, microenvironment interactions, and molecular progression. These findings hold significant promise for developing targeted therapeutic strategies [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib23)].

The application of single-cell omics and spatial profiling continues to revolutionize our understanding of cellular complexity, driving novel discoveries across developmental biology, immunology, and cancer research.

Advances in gene regulatory network analysis using single-cell omics
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Gene regulatory networks (GRNs) function as cellular control systems, where regulatory proteins such as transcription factors and genomic elements interact to modulate gene expression, ultimately shaping cellular function and development. Understanding how GRNs orchestrate biological processes and maintain cell states is fundamental to uncovering the mechanisms driving health and disease ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig2)b). The advent of single-cell multi-omics technologies has revolutionized GRN reconstruction, enabling higher-resolution insights into gene regulation across diverse biological and disease contexts.

Among recent innovations, Dictys [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib24)]∗, a dynamic GRN inference framework, integrates chromatin accessibility and gene expression data to reconstruct time-resolved and cell-type-specific GRNs. By leveraging transcription factor footprinting and stochastic modeling, Dictys has been successfully applied in developmental studies of human blood and mouse skin, offering deeper insights into dynamic gene regulation. Similarly, FigR [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib25)] connects distal cis-regulatory elements with their target genes, enabling functional GRN inference. By combining scRNA-seq and scATAC-seq data, FigR identified Domains of Regulatory Chromatin (DORCs), revealing key regulatory regions that drive immune responses.

Single-cell GRN analysis has also provided groundbreaking insights into neurological disorders. Wamsley et al. [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib26)]∗∗ utilized single-nucleus RNA and ATAC sequencing to dissect GRNs in autism spectrum disorder (ASD), identifying cell-type-specific regulatory networks influenced by both rare and common genetic variants. Their findings linked GRN dysregulation to cellular and circuit-level abnormalities in the autistic brain.

Despite these advances, scaling GRN inference methods to accommodate increasingly complex datasets remains a challenge. As single-cell and multi-omics technologies continue to evolve, further computational and experimental innovations will be essential to unlock the full potential of GRN-based discoveries in developmental biology, immunology, and disease research.

Functional perturbations at the single-cell level
-------------------------------------------------

Single-cell perturbation techniques integrate experimental interventions (e.g., CRISPR-based gene editing, chemical treatments) with single-cell resolution measurements (e.g., transcriptomics, epigenomics, proteomics) to study how specific perturbations influence cellular states and functions ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#tbl1)). These methods serve as powerful tools for dissecting gene regulatory networks (GRNs), identifying drug targets, and understanding cellular responses to environmental changes.

For instance, CROP-seq [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib27)] enables the simultaneous study of genetic perturbations and their transcriptional effects in thousands of individual cells, while DRUG-seq [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib28)] facilitates detailed transcriptional profiling of drug responses, accelerating drug discovery efforts.

Given the cost and labor-intensive nature of experimental perturbation studies, computational approaches—termed in silico perturbation methods—have emerged. These leverage single-cell multiomics data to model gene expression changes following virtual perturbations of candidate genes and providing deeper insights into gene regulation and cellular dynamics ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig2)c).

Notable examples include:
*   •CellOracle [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib29)], which reconstructs GRNs and simulates transcription factor perturbations using single-cell data. 
*   •Amrute et al. (2023) [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib30)], who used in silico RUNX1 perturbation to predict cardiac recovery, simulating knockout effects on macrophages and fibroblasts, later validated in vivo. 
*   •Quan et al. (2024) [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib31)], who applied in silico gene perturbation to uncover type-I interferon regulatory mechanisms in Parkinson's disease, identifying NFATC2 as a key regulator of neuroinflammation. 
*   •Yan et al. (2024) [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib32)], who conducted in silico perturbation of SHOX2 and MEOX2, revealing their essential roles in anterior and posterior palate development, providing insights into palatogenesis and cleft palate formation. 

Together, these studies highlight the growing impact of single-cell perturbation techniques, both experimental and computational, in dissecting disease mechanisms, investigating cellular responses, and identifying novel therapeutic targets. As these approaches continue to evolve, they promise to further redefine our understanding of gene function and regulation at the single-cell level.

Lineage trajectory analysis through single-cell omics
-----------------------------------------------------

Lineage trajectory analysis through single-cell omics has revolutionized our ability to dissect cellular trajectories and dynamic processes, enabling researchers to profile molecular changes at the single-cell level and gain deeper insights into cellular states, differentiation pathways, and molecular mechanisms underlying health and disease ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig2)d). Trajectory analysis in scRNA-seq data models the progression of cells through continuous biological processes, such as differentiation, by ordering them along a pseudo-time axis based on their gene expression profiles. Combining trajectory inference with RNA velocity further enhances our ability to reconstruct differentiation pathways, while RNA velocity itself models cellular gene expression dynamics by analyzing unspliced and spliced mRNA [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib33)]∗∗.

Deep learning, a subset of artificial intelligence in which computational models learn to recognize patterns and make predictions by processing large datasets through artificial neural networks, is increasingly being incorporated into single-cell omics analysis. ## Abstract

Lineage tracing is a valuable technique that has greatly facilitated the exploration of cell origins and behavior. With the continuous development of single-cell sequencing technology, lineage tracing technology based on the single-cell level has become an important method to study biological development. Single-cell Lineage tracing technology plays an important role in the hematological system. It can help to answer many important questions, such as the heterogeneity of hematopoietic stem cell function and structure, and the heterogeneity of malignant tumor cells in the hematological system. Many studies have been conducted to explore the field of hematology by applying this technology. This review focuses on the superiority of the emerging single-cell lineage tracing technologies of Integration barcodes, CRISPR barcoding, and base editors, and summarizes their applications in the hematology system. These studies have suggested the vast potential in unraveling complex cellular behaviors and lineage dynamics in both normal and pathological contexts.

## Background

Lineage tracing is a technique that can track all descendants of a single progenitor cell and reveal their fate trajectories. Lineage tracing can provide biological characteristics of progenitor cell progeny, such as cell expression profile characteristics, differentiation status, etc. It provides a powerful tool for cell fate mapping and is recognized as the primary method for revealing the complex connections between progenitor cells and their progeny. Originally prominent in classical developmental biology, lineage tracing is now expanding to stem cell research and modeling of cellular diversity in cancer [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR1 "Kretzschmar K, Watt FM. Lineage tracing. Cell. 2012;148(1–2):33–45."), [2](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR2 "McKenna A, Findlay GM, Gagnon JA, Horwitz MS, Schier AF, Shendure J. Whole-organism lineage tracing by combinatorial and cumulative genome editing. Science. 2016;353(6298):aaf7907.")]. It provides a unique perspective for studying cellular behavior within intact tissues or organisms. Single-cell sequencing technology allows us to resolve cellular states at the single-cell resolution level. Lineage tracing, the gold standard for cellular trajectory inference, combined with single-cell sequencing, enables single-cell lineage tracing (SCLT) to map cell lineage connectivity at single-cell resolution and is the best tool for exploring the heterogeneity of cellular differentiation [[3](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR3 "Saelens W, Cannoodt R, Todorov H, Saeys Y. A comparison of single-cell trajectory inference methods. Nat Biotechnol. 2019;37(5):547–54.")]. Hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) are a continuous source of hematopoietic and immune cells, and their clonal behavior and subclonal diversity are closely related to blood disorders, cancer treatments, and the aging process, and thus are important for advancing regenerative medicine and precision therapies. The development of SCLT technology provides a great opportunity to gain insights into how these stem cells can contribute to the hematopoietic process in both healthy and diseased states. This review briefly summarizes the evolution and development of SCLT technology, focuses on its application in hematology, and discusses its implications for the field of hematology.

## The single-cell lineage tracing techniques

### History and application of traditional lineage tracing

In the 19th century, lineage tracing was revolutionized by Charles O. Whitman and colleagues, who pioneered direct observation using light microscopy to track cell fates in invertebrate embryos [[4](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR4 "Child C. The Organization and Cell-lineage of the Ascidian Egg. By, Professor EG, Conklin I. 1905. Science. 1906;23(583):340-4.")]. Direct observation offers advantages such as speed, ease of use, and non-invasiveness. This method has also been successfully applied to zebrafish embryos due to their transparency [[5](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR5 "Kimmel CB, Warga RM, Schilling TF. Origin and organization of the zebrafish fate map. Development. 1990;108(4):581–94.")]. However, continuous observation of intact organisms requires transparent embryos with a limited number of cells, which complicates the study of complex tissues or post-implantation mammalian embryos. When direct tracing isn’t possible, alternative labeling techniques are necessary, such as using dyes and enzymes [[6](#ref-CR6 "Kimmel CB, Law RD. Cell lineage of zebrafish blastomeres. III. Clonal analyses of the blastula and gastrula stages. Dev Biol. 1985;108(1):94–101."),[7](#ref-CR7 "Keller RE. Vital dye mapping of the gastrula and Neurula of Xenopus laevis. II. Prospective areas and morphogenetic movements of the deep layer. Dev Biol. 1976;51(1):118–37."),[8](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR8 "Weisblat DA, Sawyer RT, Stent GS. Cell lineage analysis by intracellular injection of a tracer enzyme. Science. 1978;202(4374):1295–8.")], cross-species transplantation [[9](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR9 "Le Douarin NM, Teillet MA. Experimental analysis of the migration and differentiation of neuroblasts of the autonomic nervous system and of neurectodermal mesenchymal derivatives, using a biological cell marking technique. Dev Biol. 1974;41(1):162–84.")], inducible activation of reporter gene expression through recombinase-mediated mechanisms [[10](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR10 "Dymecki SM, Tomasiewicz H. Using Flp-recombinase to characterize expansion of Wnt1-expressing neural progenitors in the mouse. Dev Biol. 1998;201(1):57–65."), [11](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR11 "Zinyk DL, Mercer EH, Harris E, Anderson DJ, Joyner AL. Fate mapping of the mouse midbrain-hindbrain constriction using a site-specific recombination system. Curr Biol. 1998;8(11):665–8.")], and insertion of exogenous DNA sequences [[12](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR12 "Walsh C, Cepko CL. Widespread dispersion of neuronal clones across functional regions of the cerebral cortex. Science. 1992;255(5043):434–40."), [13](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR13 "Porter SN, Baker LC, Mittelman D, Porteus MH. Lentiviral and targeted cellular barcoding reveals ongoing clonal dynamics of cell lines in vitro and in vivo. Genome Biol. 2014;15(5):R75.")]. Advances in molecular genetics have enabled more precise cell labeling through direct transfection or viral infection. Fluorescent proteins, often used as “molecular dyes,” are common for tracking cell behavior. However, traditional lineage tracing methods have limited resolution. For example, the control of fluorescent proteins by site-specific recombinases [[14](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR14 "Holland EC, Varmus HE. Basic fibroblast growth factor induces cell migration and proliferation after glia-specific gene transfer in mice. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A. 1998;95(3):1218–23.")], such as *Cre*, is typically regulated by cell-type-specific promoters, which restricts the fluorescent signal to a group of cells, reducing the precision of tracking individual cells.

### Development and advantages of Single-cell lineage tracing

In the traditional approach to lineage tracing, all target cells are marked with the same label, leading to a lack of detailed information about the individual biological behaviors of each cell. Breakthroughs in single-cell sequencing have provided a way to break through the bottleneck, but to comprehensively track cell lines in complex multicellular systems, individual cells, and their progeny must be meticulously and sequentially labeled throughout numerous cell divisions. This labeling process must be performed without disturbing normal developmental processes. In addition, the selected cellular markers should accumulate irreversibly over time, a critical aspect that facilitates the subsequent reconstruction of the lineage tree and an essential component of a comprehensive lineage-tracing effort [[2](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR2 "McKenna A, Findlay GM, Gagnon JA, Horwitz MS, Schier AF, Shendure J. Whole-organism lineage tracing by combinatorial and cumulative genome editing. Science. 2016;353(6298):aaf7907.")]. To achieve appropriate labeling and achieve higher-resolution genealogical tracing, the non-invasive study of gene fate mapping [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR1 "Kretzschmar K, Watt FM. Lineage tracing. Cell. 2012;148(1–2):33–45.")] has gained widespread use (Table [1](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#Tab1); Fig. [1](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#Fig1)).

#### Multicolor labeling systems

Researchers have implemented strategies involving multiple *flox* sites and fluorescent proteins in specific combinations. Exemplified by systems like Brainbow and Confetti, these techniques create a diverse palette of colors that enable the discrimination of different cells upon activation of the *Cre* recombinase. Brainbow, in particular, has been a valuable source of biological insights, contributing to the understanding of various domains such as neuronal connectivity patterns [[15](#ref-CR15 "Livet J, Weissman TA, Kang H, Draft RW, Lu J, Bennis RA, et al. #### Integration barcodes

To further improve the resolution of lineage tracing, DNA barcoding techniques have been introduced, utilizing DNA fragments with extensive sequence variations to label individual cells. The discovery of retroviral vectors has provided a means to introduce new genetic material into HSCs [[26](#ref-CR26 "Williams DA, Lemischka IR, Nathan DG, Mulligan RC. Introduction of new genetic material into pluripotent Haematopoietic stem cells of the mouse. Nature. 1984;310(5977):476–80."),[27](#ref-CR27 "Joyner A, Keller G, Phillips RA, Bernstein A. Retrovirus transfer of a bacterial gene into mouse Haematopoietic progenitor cells. Nature. 1983;305(5934):556–8."),[28](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR28 "Miller AD, Eckner RJ, Jolly DJ, Friedmann T, Verma IM. Expression of a retrovirus encoding human HPRT in mice. Science. 1984;225(4662):630–2.")], offering an approach for analyzing hematopoietic cell clones. Retroviral libraries containing DNA barcodes enable the simultaneous labeling of thousands of cells, thereby enhancing the quantity and precision of lineage tracing information [[29](#ref-CR29 "Lu R, Neff NF, Quake SR, Weissman IL. Tracking single hematopoietic stem cells in vivo using high-throughput sequencing in conjunction with viral genetic barcoding. Nat Biotechnol. 2011;29(10):928–33."),[30](#ref-CR30 "Naik SH, Schumacher TN, Perié L. Cellular barcoding: a technical appraisal. Exp Hematol. 2014;42(8):598–608."),[31](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR31 "Weinreb C, Rodriguez-Fraticelli A, Camargo FD, Klein AM. Lineage tracing on transcriptional landscapes links state to fate during differentiation. Science. 2020;367(6479).")].

In recipients of HSC transplantation mediated by retroviral transduction, the differentiation of distinct hematopoietic stem cell-derived clones can be achieved by considering retroviral integration sites as unique clonal markers [[32](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR32 "Capel B, Hawley RG, Mintz B. Long- and short-lived murine hematopoietic stem cell clones individually identified with retroviral integration markers. Blood. 1990;75(12):2267–70."), [33](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR33 "Jordan CT, Lemischka IR. Clonal and systemic analysis of long-term hematopoiesis in the mouse. Genes Dev. 1990;4(2):220–32.")]. A retroviral plasmid library was developed, comprising vectors incorporating a variable, random sequence tag, or “barcode.” Upon stable chromosomal integration, this barcode imparts a unique, discernible, and inheritable identifier into the genome, enabling the long-term tracking of clonal descendants originating from the host cell [[24](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR24 "Gerrits A, Dykstra B, Kalmykowa OJ, Klauke K, Verovskaya E, Broekhuis MJ, et al. Cellular barcoding tool for clonal analysis in the hematopoietic system. Blood. 2010;115(13):2610–8.")].

Through this technique, distinct subpopulations within the primitive hematopoietic hierarchy could be selectively isolated through fluorescence-activated cell sorting from recipients subjected to transplants involving barcoded HSCs. Subsequent analysis facilitated the examination of clonal relationships between these cellular compartments. Compared to previous studies involving single-cell transplants, the volume of information obtainable from a single transplant recipient was substantially enhanced. Moreover, such data served as a valuable resource for validating or augmenting established theoretical frameworks and quantitative models elucidating the clonal dynamics inherent to HSCs [[34](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR34 "Roeder I, Loeffler M. A novel dynamic model of hematopoietic stem cell organization based on the concept of within-tissue plasticity. Exp Hematol. 2002;30(8):853–61."), [35](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR35 "Roeder I, Horn K, Sieburg HB, Cho R, Muller-Sieburg C, Loeffler M. Characterization and quantification of clonal heterogeneity among hematopoietic stem cells: a model-based approach. Blood. 2008;112(13):4874–83.")].

Despite the advantages offered by viral barcoding methodologies, the use of retroviruses faces two significant challenges. Firstly, retroviruses are limited to labeling actively dividing cells, restricting their applicability to quiescent or non-proliferating cell populations. Secondly, retroviral vectors are prone to spontaneous silencing, potentially hindering the consistent maintenance of lineage markers over time. Additionally, it’s crucial to recognize that spontaneous cell fusion phenomena occur in various tissue contexts [[36](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR36 "Alvarez-Dolado M, Pardal R, Garcia-Verdugo JM, Fike JR, Lee HO, Pfeffer K, et al. Fusion of bone-marrow-derived cells with purkinje neurons, cardiomyocytes and hepatocytes. Nature. 2003;425(6961):968–73."), [37](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR37 "Wang X, Willenbring H, Akkari Y, Torimaru Y, Foster M, Al-Dhalimy M, et al. Cell fusion is the principal source of bone-marrow-derived hepatocytes. Nature. 2003;422(6934):897–901.")]. These fusion events can enable the transfer of lineage markers between cells of different lineages [[38](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR38 "Ying QL, Nichols J, Evans EP, Smith AG. Changing potency by spontaneous fusion. Nature. 2002;416(6880):545–8."), [39](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR39 "Terada N, Hamazaki T, Oka M, Hoki M, Mastalerz DM, Nakano Y, et al. Bone marrow cells adopt the phenotype of other cells by spontaneous cell fusion. Nature. 2002;416(6880):542–5.")], inadvertently leading to inaccurate conclusions about transdifferentiating processes [[40](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR40 "Weissman IL. Normal and neoplastic stem cells. Novartis Found Symp. 2005;265:35–50; discussion– 4, 92– 7.")].

Polylox represents an artificial DNA recombination locus that enables endogenous barcoding using the *Cre-loxP* recombination system [[41](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR41 "Sternberg N, Hamilton D. A recent breakthrough in lineage tracing involves the development of base editors, introducing informative sites to document cell division events. Indels and mutations induced by CRISPR-based genome editing offer faster mutation rates, allowing for the recording of more mitotic divisions and the construction of more detailed cell lineage trees [[61](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR61 "Liu K, Deng S, Ye C, Yao Z, Wang J, Gong H, et al. Mapping single-cell-resolution cell phylogeny reveals cell population dynamics during organ development. Nat Methods. 2021;18(12):1506–14.")]. Researchers applied this technique to Drosophila melanogaster, obtaining an average of more than 20 mutations on a three-kilobase-pair barcoding sequence in early-adult cells. Leveraging these barcoding mutations, high-quality cell phylogenetic trees were generated, each comprising several thousand internal nodes with 84–93% median bootstrap support. The resulting cell phylogenies facilitated a population genetic analysis, estimating the longitudinal dynamics of the number of actively dividing parental cells in each organ throughout development. The Np dynamics revealed the trajectory of cell births and provided insights into the balance of symmetric and asymmetric cell division [[61](/articles/10.1186/s12967-025-06318-4#ref-CR61 "Liu K, Deng S, Ye C, Yao Z, Wang J, Gong H, et al. Mapping single-cell-resolution cell phylogeny reveals cell population dynamics during organ development. Nat Methods. 2021;18(12):1506–14.")].

enriched_points.expanded_content: Single-cell lineage tracing techniques enable tracking cellular origins, differentiation pathways, and fate trajectories, providing insights into developmental processes and tissue formation.

Single-cell omics technologies dissect molecular interactions and regulatory networks that orchestrate development, enhancing our understanding of cellular dynamics and differentiation during embryogenesis.

Single-cell omics facilitate the identification of cellular heterogeneity and gene regulatory disruptions in developmental disorders, revealing potential biomarkers and therapeutic targets for conditions like autism and congenital anomalies.
